sections.title: How to use this site
sections.html: Use the search box or expand the tree on the left. How-to pages provide downloadable guides. Troubleshooting pages provide concise symptoms and suggestions.

children.title: Camera
children.summary: Camera troubleshooting workflow and common issue cases.

sections.title: Details
sections.html: Camera troubleshooting workflow content will be added here.

children.title: Camera Offline
children.summary: Troubleshooting steps for camera offline or intermittent disconnection issues.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
1. First check whether the network cable is defective. Connect the faulty camera and another normally powered camera to the same switch using the same network cable for comparison. If the cable is confirmed to be defective, replace it with a known good cable.
  2. If the cable is normal, check whether the switch load is too high or whether the PoE power budget is insufficient.
  3. Check whether the UPS power supply is unstable.
  4. Check whether the network port is damaged or short-circuited.
  5. If the issue may be caused by a software program exception, collect the required information and send it to HQ for analysis.

children.title: Black Screen
children.summary: Troubleshooting steps for black screen or live view failure.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
1. If live view shows a black screen or fails, check whether it is caused by a browser/plugin issue. Uninstall and reinstall the plugin.
  2. Check whether the maximum connection limit has been exceeded.
  3. For 1/2/3 series devices, the maximum number of connections is 6. For 4/5/6/7/8 series devices and PTZ cameras, the maximum number of connections is 20. Live view may fail after the limit is exceeded.

children.title: Blurry Preview
children.summary: Troubleshooting steps for blurry camera preview images.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
1. Check whether the network bandwidth is congested. Insufficient bandwidth may cause video lag and blurry images.
  2. Check whether Video Encoding is set to H.264+ or H.265+. If so, disable H.264+/H.265+.
  3. Check whether the audio/video parameters are reasonable. Set Bitrate Type to constant, set Image Quality to High, and set Max. Bitrate to 8 Mbps as a reference for a 2 MP device.
  4. Set the stream protocol to TCP.

children.title: Night Blur/Ghosting
children.summary: Troubleshooting steps for blurry or ghosting images at night or in low-light scenes.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
1. For general low-light ghosting, set Supplement Light Brightness Control to manual and adjust brightness to 100. Improve the ambient lighting conditions where possible.

  1. If ghosting is caused by slow shutter speed, increase the shutter speed or reduce the exposure time, for example to 1/50. Note that too short an exposure time may darken the image.

  1. If ghosting is caused by temporal noise reduction, set Digital Noise Reduction to Expert Mode and reduce Time DNR Level to 30-40. Image noise may increase; you can raise Space DNR Level to 60-70 as needed.

  1. Reduce sharpness to around 30 and increase contrast to 60-70 to reduce noise interference.

  1. If WDR is enabled in a low-light scene and causes ghosting, disable WDR or set WDR to Auto. WDR is intended for strong-light or backlight scenes.

children.title: Missing Recording
children.summary: Troubleshooting steps for missing segments in continuous SD card recording.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
If continuous recording is configured but recording is interrupted during certain time periods while the SD card status shows no abnormality, use the following checks.

  1. First confirm the SD card specifications and compatibility.
  2. Then try adjusting the I-frame interval or video frame rate. As a reference, set the I-frame interval to about twice the frame rate. The issue may be related to insufficient recording buffer.

children.title: OSD Time Jump
children.summary: Troubleshooting steps for camera OSD time jumping forward or backward.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
1. Confirm whether the NTP parameters are configured correctly and provide a screenshot of the NTP settings.
  2. Confirm whether the device is connected to multiple time synchronization sources, such as multiple NVRs, DVRs, or platforms.
  3. Confirm whether the configured time zone matches the local time zone and provide the time zone configuration.
  4. Confirm whether daylight saving time is enabled and provide a screenshot of the DST settings.
  5. Confirm whether there was any manual time synchronization caused by user operation.

children.title: IPC Offline
children.summary: Troubleshooting guidance for IPC disconnection from an NVR, especially power-related disconnection.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
1. Check whether the IPC is powered off when it goes offline, and whether it only goes offline in a specific environment. For example, when the IPC enables infrared or the heating module, the required power increases and may cause the IPC to go offline. Disable high-power-consumption functions as appropriate, or power the IPC separately.

The following checks can help determine whether the issue is power-related:

  • If both the PoE indicator and the IPC power indicator are off, it is a power supply issue.
  • Compare the channel PoE port power on the NVR local interface with the IPC PoE power. If the difference is very large, it is a power supply issue.
  • Power the PoE camera separately and add the camera again. If the image is displayed normally, it is a power supply issue.

children.title: Sub-Stream Issues
children.summary: Sub-stream access limits and stream selection behavior for IPC access.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
For IPC sub-stream access, the bitrate must not exceed 2 Mbps and the resolution must not exceed 720p. In this case, reduce the sub-stream bitrate and resolution.

When a third-party IPC is added, the NVR determines the stream type. The default priority is H.264 > MPEG4 > MJPEG. When the encoding format is the same, streams are sorted by the maximum supported resolution.

For example, if the IPC main stream is MPEG4 1080p and the sub-stream is H.264 720p, the NVR will use 720p as the main stream. The MPEG4 1080p sub-stream exceeds the resolution limit and cannot be added. When setting the main stream type, use the priority H.264 > MPEG4 > MJPEG.

children.title: HDD Issues
children.summary: HDD troubleshooting steps including SMART test, SATA cable checks, and power supply checks.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
When an HDD issue occurs, perform a SMART test first.

SMART Test

If the SMART test shows an abnormal result, replace the HDD. The SMART test can be performed on the NVR local interface. If the device has not created a RAID array, the SMART test can also be performed from the web interface.

If self-evaluation fails, the HDD has not passed the SMART test. Pay close attention to Reallocated Sector Count. If the value is 1, it means the HDD is currently in the worst condition. In this case, replace the HDD, perform SMART tests on the remaining disks, and replace any problematic HDDs in time.

SATA Cable Check

Cross-check the SATA cables. For example, swap the third cable with the fourth cable, and the first cable with the fifth cable. If the offline HDD slot changes after swapping the SATA cable, replace the SATA cable.

Power Supply Check

Check whether the HDD is spinning and whether the HDD indicator on the front panel is lit.

If the HDD is not spinning and the HDD indicator on the front panel is not lit, it can be determined that the HDD power supply is abnormal.

children.title: Smart Event Search
children.summary: Conditions required for Smart Event Search for motion detection, line crossing, and intrusion events.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
Smart Event Search can search the following event types: motion detection, line crossing, and intrusion detection.

On the device local interface, Save Camera VCA Data must be enabled in the storage module. For line crossing and intrusion detection, Dual-VCA must be enabled. After it is enabled, Smart Event Search can be used regardless of whether the event itself is enabled.

Motion detection is special. The motion detection event must be enabled on the device, and Enable Dynamic Analysis for Motion must also be enabled, but Dual-VCA does not need to be enabled.

Information Collection

  • Confirm whether Save Camera VCA Data is enabled on the local interface.

GUI:

  • Confirm whether the motion detection event is enabled, and whether Enable Dynamic Analysis for Motion is enabled.

  • Confirm whether Dual-VCA is enabled on the camera. In most cases, it is not enabled on the camera side, which causes intrusion detection and line crossing to fail.

children.title: Missing Recording
children.summary: Common causes of missing recordings on Hikvision NVRs.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
Hikvision NVRs do not have a function to actively delete recordings.

Common situations include:

  • When configuring a group, the channel was not selected.

  • Recording loss caused by multiple devices synchronizing time to the NVR.

If multiple platforms and multiple devices are connected for time synchronization, recording loss may occur. Advise the customer to disable time synchronization from other devices, then check and reconfigure the settings.

  • Recording loss caused by expire time / overwrite settings.

If expiry time is configured, recordings will expire. If HDD overwrite is enabled, when the disk is full, the HDD will start overwriting recordings from the earliest recording log.

children.title: Preview/Playback Failed
children.summary: Troubleshooting guidance for remote preview or playback failure and black screen issues.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
It is recommended to switch the protocol first.

During NVR remote preview, the protocol can be selected from TCP, UDP, and Multicast. If the user has high real-time requirements, use UDP. If the user has high integrity requirements, use TCP. Try switching the protocol first.

Sometimes the router or PC firewall restricts TCP connections. Try increasing the TCP wait time on the router, canceling TCP blocking, or directly switching to UDP.

If switching the protocol does not resolve the issue:

  • Uninstall the plugin, log in to the device web interface again, and reinstall the plugin.
  • Reduce the number of users pulling streams.
  • Check the antivirus software or firewall blocking records. Disable the antivirus software or firewall and compare the result.

children.title: System Time
children.summary: Troubleshooting guidance for NVR time/date staying at 1970, timezone changes, and PC time synchronization timezone mismatch.

sections.title: Details
sections.html:
Time and Date Always Stay at 1970-01-01

Button battery failure: The button battery has a service life. When the battery is depleted, it must be replaced. This is especially common for devices that have been kept in stock for a long time.

The function of the button battery is to provide power and form a minimal time synchronization system. When the NVR is powered off, the clock can still keep running and maintain accurate time. Therefore, in this situation, the time can usually be saved manually, but after reboot it changes back to 1970.

If the time cannot be saved correctly and always stays at 1970, the crystal oscillator may have failed.

NVR Timezone Changes from a Specific Value to GMT+00:00

The timezone changes from a specific value to GMT+00:00.

Check the NVR system log to see whether the timezone was modified by a remote IP address. Then use the IP address to identify the modification source.

PC Time Synchronization Causes NVR Timezone Mismatch

After synchronizing the PC time from the NVR web interface, the NVR timezone information is inconsistent with the PC timezone.

Disable Adjust for daylight saving time automatically on the PC. If daylight saving time needs to be used on the NVR, configure it on the NVR DST page.
